title: The Fiscal Cost of the Sprawl Paradox
text:
If the True Urban Core framework exposes a measurement problem — official city populations overstating functional urban density — then this analysis exposes the downstream cost: federal transportation investment is systematically misallocated along the same fault line.
The OKI region (the 8-county Ohio-Kentucky-Indiana area surrounding Cincinnati) offers an unusually clean test case. The Ohio River bisects a continuous urban fabric along a state boundary, creating the same official-vs.-actual boundary distortion examined in the main paper. Within this region, we can match tract-level fiscal productivity against tract-level federal infrastructure investment and ask: are the places generating the most public revenue receiving their proportional share of public spending?
The answer is no — by a factor of roughly 17.

title: The Fiscal Cost of the Sprawl Paradox
text:
If the True Urban Core framework exposes a measurement problem — official city populations overstating functional urban density — then this analysis exposes the downstream cost: federal transportation investment is systematically misallocated along the same fault line.
The OKI region (the 8-county Ohio-Kentucky-Indiana area surrounding Cincinnati) offers an unusually clean test case. The Ohio River bisects a continuous urban fabric along a state boundary, creating the same official-vs.-actual boundary distortion examined in the main paper. Within this region, we can match tract-level fiscal productivity against tract-level federal infrastructure investment and ask: are the places generating the most public revenue receiving their proportional share of public spending?
The answer is no — by a factor of roughly 17.

title: The Fiscal Cost of the Sprawl Paradox
text:
Data and Methodology

Fiscal Productivity
We estimate property tax productivity at the Census tract level using a revenue proxy:
\[\text{Revenue}_{i} \approx \text{Median Home Value}_i \times \text{Housing Units}_i \times 0.0125\]
This approximation applies a uniform 1.25% effective tax rate to the total estimated residential stock. It captures the relative fiscal productivity of tracts — the ratio of urban to sprawl productivity — rather than precise absolute figures.


Federal Investment
Transportation investment is measured using federal award data from USASpending.gov, with awards geocoded to Census block groups and aggregated to tracts. We focus on DOT-sourced awards (CFDA prefixes 20.xxx) to isolate transportation-specific investment.


Density Classification
Tracts are classified into three density tiers consistent with the TUC framework:








Tier
Density Threshold
Description




Urban
≥ 5,000 ppsm
Continuous built fabric; transit-viable


Suburban
1,000–5,000 ppsm
Mixed residential; auto-dependent


Sprawl
< 1,000 ppsm
Low-density; high infrastructure-per-capita cost

title: The Fiscal Cost of the Sprawl Paradox
text:
Fiscal Productivity by Density Class
Urban tracts generate approximately 17 times more estimated property tax revenue per acre than sprawl-class tracts. Yet the federal investment ranking runs in the opposite direction: suburban tracts receive the most investment per acre, while urban tracts receive the least.
This inversion is not coincidental. It reflects the structure of federal transportation programs, which historically fund lane-miles rather than people-per-acre. A highway interchange in a low-density suburb consumes more acreage — and therefore more federal dollars — than the same investment in urban infrastructure that serves a vastly larger population within the same footprint.

title: The Fiscal Cost of the Sprawl Paradox
text:
Spatial Distribution of ROI
The spatial pattern is striking but unsurprising: the highest revenue-per-acre tracts cluster in the Cincinnati urban core, Covington, and Newport — precisely the area that the main paper identifies as functionally continuous urban fabric despite being split across official municipal and state boundaries. The lowest-productivity tracts radiate outward into the exurban counties that nonetheless represent large shares of the region’s total road network.

title: The Fiscal Cost of the Sprawl Paradox
text:
The Cross-Subsidy Structure
The combined picture — high urban fiscal productivity, low urban infrastructure investment — implies a structural cross-subsidy flowing from dense cores to low-density peripheries. This is not unique to Cincinnati. It is the standard operating condition of American metropolitan public finance:

Urban tracts generate the majority of the property tax base and, via agglomeration, drive the wage and sales tax base.
Infrastructure programs are structured around vehicle-miles and lane-miles, which scale with land area rather than population density.
Low-density development generates large maintenance liabilities — approximately $10,000 per lane-mile annually — that are partly offset by transfers from higher-productivity jurisdictions.

The implication for the True Urban Core framework is direct: cities that score high on official population rankings but low on TUC-adjusted urbanicity (Indianapolis, Columbus, Phoenix) are extracting cross-subsidies from the dense regions they nominally outrank. The “Sprawl Paradox” in population measurement has an exact fiscal analog in transportation spending.

title: The Fiscal Cost of the Sprawl Paradox
text:
Methodological Notes

Revenue estimates assume a uniform 1.25% effective tax rate. Actual rates vary substantially across Hamilton, Kenton, Campbell, and the five other OKI counties; the proxy is suitable for cross-sectional comparison within the region but not for absolute revenue projection.
Federal award geocoding maps point awards to the nearest road segment using Overture Maps data, then aggregates to Census geographies. Awards with county-level precision only are excluded.
Maintenance liability estimates follow AASHTO’s standard $10,000/lane-mile/year figure, applied to total road mileage per block group from OpenStreetMap.

title: The Fiscal Cost of the Sprawl Paradox
text:
References

Federal Highway Administration (2024). Highway Statistics Series.
USASpending.gov (2023). Federal Award Data, DOT Transportation Programs.
U.S. Census Bureau (2022). American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates.
Overture Maps Foundation (2025). Roads Layer: Technical Documentation.
Strong Towns (2021). The Cost of Car Dependency.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
text: US city population rankings are artifacts of 20th-century boundary consolidations, not measures of urban intensity. Indianapolis, Columbus, and Louisville absorbed their suburbs wholesale; Boston and San Francisco could not. The result is a ranking system that rewards administrative reach over actual density. This analysis introduces the True Urban Core (TUC) — the contiguous population residing in Census tracts exceeding 10,000 persons per square mile, the threshold associated with transit viability and walkable neighborhood services. When applied uniformly, TUC reranking is dramatic: Phoenix, Indianapolis, and Louisville collapse; Boston, San Francisco, and Washington D.C. rise to the top tier. The analysis also decomposes the “consolidation premium” — the share of a city’s official population attributable to absorbed low-density territory — across all 100 largest MSAs.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
text: US city population rankings are artifacts of 20th-century boundary consolidations, not measures of urban intensity. Indianapolis, Columbus, and Louisville absorbed their suburbs wholesale; Boston and San Francisco could not. The result is a ranking system that rewards administrative reach over actual density. This analysis introduces the True Urban Core (TUC) — the contiguous population residing in Census tracts exceeding 10,000 persons per square mile, the threshold associated with transit viability and walkable neighborhood services. When applied uniformly, TUC reranking is dramatic: Phoenix, Indianapolis, and Louisville collapse; Boston, San Francisco, and Washington D.C. rise to the top tier. The analysis also decomposes the “consolidation premium” — the share of a city’s official population attributable to absorbed low-density territory — across all 100 largest MSAs.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
section: I. The Problem: When Boundaries Lie
text:
I. The Problem: When Boundaries Lie
In the United States, a “City” is a political entity, not a geographic one. Cities like Indianapolis (361 sq mi) and Louisville (380 sq mi) captured entire counties under a single population count through 20th-century city-county consolidations. Conversely, cities like Boston (48 sq mi) and San Francisco (46 sq mi) are physically constrained by geography and failed consolidation attempts.
This creates a ranking system that rewards sprawl over intensity. Columbus, OH (pop. ~900k) officially outranks San Francisco (~800k), despite SF having nearly five times the population density. From a policy standpoint, this matters: transit funding, housing grants, and infrastructure investment often scale with official city population — allocating resources on the basis of a measurement that systematically undercounts genuine urban density and overcounts suburban sprawl.
The TUC framework is a corrective. By anchoring the definition of “urban” in density rather than administrative history, it provides a consistent, reproducible basis for comparing cities across different political geographies.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
text:
II. Methodology: The 10,000 ppsm Threshold
We define the True Urban Core as the contiguous population residing in Census tracts exceeding 10,000 persons per square mile (ppsm).
The 10,000 ppsm threshold is grounded in the urban economics literature (Pushkarev & Zupan 1977) as the minimum density at which frequent mass transit becomes financially viable and walkable commercial corridors can support themselves. It corresponds roughly to a tight grid of mid-rise residential buildings — the neighborhood texture of Boston’s Back Bay, San Francisco’s Mission District, or Manhattan south of 96th Street.
Data: 2022 ACS 5-Year Estimates at the Census tract level. Population density is calculated as tract population divided by land area in square miles. Tracts meeting the threshold are included in the TUC; official city population benchmarks are sourced from Census place-level estimates.
The 5K Analysis page applies the same methodology at the 5,000 ppsm threshold — the Census Bureau’s standard definition of “urbanized” — for comparison.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
section: III. Case Study: The Cincinnati–NKY Urban Continuity
text:
III. Case Study: The Cincinnati–NKY Urban Continuity
The discrepancy between political boundaries and urban reality is most visible in river-crossing metropolitan areas. In the Cincinnati region, the “City” officially stops at the Ohio River. But the urban fabric continues seamlessly into Covington and Newport, Kentucky — neighborhoods that share Cincinnati’s street grid, building types, and commuting patterns but fall in a different state, county, and for transit purposes, a different agency jurisdiction.
Simultaneously, large portions of the official “Cincinnati” boundary in the north and west contain lower-density residential areas that function as suburbs by every meaningful metric. They are in the city on paper; they are not in the urban core in practice.
The map below illustrates this directly: red shading marks the True Urban Core (tracts ≥ 10,000 ppsm or ≥ 4,000 housing units per sq mi); blue outlines show official municipal limits.




Blue outlines = official city limits. Red fill = True Urban Core (≥10k ppsm or ≥4k units/sq mi).


The Ohio River boundary severs a functionally unified urban area into two jurisdictions with separate transit agencies, separate tax bases, and separate political representation — a fragmentation whose costs are documented separately in the Transit Fragmentation research.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
text:
IV. The Great Re-ranking
When we strip away low-density territory and count only True Urban residents, the hierarchy of American cities shifts dramatically.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
text:
V. Interpreting the Results

The Consolidation Mirage
Cities like Indianapolis and Louisville exhibit urbanicity indices below 2% — fewer than 2 in 100 residents live in a neighborhood with urban-level density. Their national rankings are statistical artifacts of administrative consolidation, not evidence of urban vitality. From a policy standpoint, directing transit investment, housing policy, or agglomeration-premium analysis to these cities on the basis of their official populations systematically misallocates resources.


The Density Powerhouses
Boston (+10) and San Francisco (+9) show the largest rank gains. When sprawl is removed from the comparison, these cities reveal themselves as far more significant urban centers than their officially-larger neighbors in the South and Midwest. Their density is not a quirk of geography to be explained away — it is the feature.


The Fiscal Implication
The ranking distortion is not merely academic. Because federal transportation and housing programs often use official city or MSA population as a basis for formula allocations, cities with high consolidation premiums systematically receive infrastructure spending disproportionate to their actual urban density. The Fiscal ROI analysis documents this directly in the OKI region: urban tracts generate 17 times more property tax revenue per acre than sprawl tracts while receiving significantly less federal transportation investment per acre.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
text:
VI. Conclusion
The True Urban Core reranking is not an argument against large cities. It is an argument for measuring what we claim to measure. When urban economists, transit planners, and housing researchers speak of “city population,” they implicitly mean people living in conditions that generate urban externalities — density-dependent agglomeration, walkable access, transit viability. Official boundaries capture something else: the outcomes of 20th-century political negotiations over annexation and consolidation.
The TUC metric provides a consistent, theoretically grounded alternative. Its application reveals that the United States has far fewer truly dense urban places than official rankings suggest, concentrated primarily on the Northeast corridor and Pacific Coast — and that the policy implications of this misclassification compound across transit funding, housing investment, and fiscal geography.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity Through Density
text:
References

U.S. Census Bureau (2022). American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates.
Pushkarev, B., & Zupan, J. M. (1977). Public Transportation and Land Use Policy. Indiana University Press.
Glaeser, E. L. (2011). Triumph of the City. Penguin Press.
Albouy, D., & Ehrlich, G. (2018). Housing productivity and the social cost of land-use restrictions. Journal of Urban Economics, 107, 101–120.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity (5,000 PPSM Threshold)
text: US city population rankings are historically determined by arbitrary political boundaries, many of which were expanded through 20th-century consolidations (e.g., Indianapolis, Columbus, Louisville). These boundaries often include vast tracts of low-density suburban sprawl, creating a “Sprawl Paradox” where cities with millions of residents are functionally less urban than smaller, denser neighbors. This paper re-evaluates US city rankings using the True Urban Density threshold of 5,000 people per square mile (ppsm), the Census standard for urban areas. We find that even at this more inclusive threshold, traditional rankings shift significantly, revealing the “suburban” nature of many high-ranking US municipalities.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity (5,000 PPSM Threshold)
text: US city population rankings are historically determined by arbitrary political boundaries, many of which were expanded through 20th-century consolidations (e.g., Indianapolis, Columbus, Louisville). These boundaries often include vast tracts of low-density suburban sprawl, creating a “Sprawl Paradox” where cities with millions of residents are functionally less urban than smaller, denser neighbors. This paper re-evaluates US city rankings using the True Urban Density threshold of 5,000 people per square mile (ppsm), the Census standard for urban areas. We find that even at this more inclusive threshold, traditional rankings shift significantly, revealing the “suburban” nature of many high-ranking US municipalities.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity (5,000 PPSM Threshold)
text:
I. Methodology: The 5k Threshold
We redefine a “City” based on functional urban density. We set the threshold at 5,000 people per square mile.

Rationale: 5,000 ppsm is the U.S. Census Bureau’s baseline for an area to be considered “Urbanized.” It typically represents neighborhoods with continuous built-up fabric, including small-lot single-family homes, duplexes, and local commercial corridors.
Data: We analyze 2022 ACS 5-Year Estimates at the Census Tract level for the top 50 metropolitan regions.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity (5,000 PPSM Threshold)
section: II. Case Study: Mapping the Top 10 Metropolitan Cores
text:
II. Case Study: Mapping the Top 10 Metropolitan Cores

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity (5,000 PPSM Threshold)
text:
IV. Conclusion
Even at the more lenient 5,000 ppsm threshold, the “Sprawl Paradox” persists. Cities like Indianapolis and Louisville remain significantly less urbanized than their coastal counterparts. This analysis suggests that US city rankings are fundamentally flawed as a measure of urban significance, as they prioritize administrative reach over actual population intensity.

title: The Sprawl Paradox: Redefining American Urbanity (5,000 PPSM Threshold)
text:
V. References

U.S. Census Bureau (2022). American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates.
Glaeser, E. L. (2011). Triumph of the City. Penguin Press.
